coredata.dc:title: Portable and real-time detection for tetracycline antibiotics using europium-doped LDH gel intercalated graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Ruiyu
Liu, Jiawei
Ma, Yongpeng
Li, Hongbo
Hai, Xiaoqin
Niu, Jiaxiao
Guo, Xiaoli
Di, Xin
coredata.dc:description: Tetracyclines (TCs) residues pose a significant threat to the aquatic environment and human health, therefore this study aims to develop a simple, rapid, and sensitive TCs detection method. Herein, a dual-responsive gel probe (LDH-CES@N) was designed, consisting of the intercalation of graphene quantum dots into europium-doped layered double hydroxide (LDH). In the presence of TCs, the as-prepared probe exhibited dual emission fluorescence at 504 nm and 616 nm due to the synergistic effect of aggregation-induced emission and antenna effect. Meanwhile, the density functional theory was employed to validate the mechanism underlying TC-induced electron transfer from graphene quantum dots. The dual-signal response fluorescence probe has excellent detection ability of oxytetracycline, including a wide detection range (0–60 μM), low detection limit (0.145 μM), and rapid response time (120 s). Furthermore, combined with the smartphone, a portable and real-time detection platform was established for the visual detection of oxytetracycline in tap water and honey samples with desirable recovery rates (97.8 %-105.4 %). Therefore, this work provides a new strategy for fluorescence detection of trace pollutants, demonstrating considerable practical application potential.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tetracyclines
Fluorescence
Layered double hydroxide
Aggregation-induced emission
Antenna effect

Visual detection of OTC using a 96-well plate
Selectivity and anti-interference performance for TCs detection
Smartphone-assisted visual detection of OTC in real sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The preparation of LDH-CES@N and the sensing mechanism for dual-signal and visual detection of OTC.
Optimization of extraction conditions: the effect of moles of sodium citrate (a), moles of Eu3+ (b), volume of ethanol to MPTS (c), and mass of the N-iso (d).

FT-IR spectra (a), XRD spectrum (b), and XPS spectrum (c) to each step of material synthesis. The high-resolution C 1 s (d), O 1 s (e), and N 1 s (f) XPS spectrum of LDH-C. (g) The high-resolution Eu 3d XPS spectrum of LDH-CE. (h) The high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum of LDH-CES. (i) The high-resolution N 1 s XPS spectrum of LDH-CES@N.
SEM images of (a) LDH-C, (b) LDH-CE, (c) SEM-EDS mapping of LDH-CE, (d) LDH-CES, (e) LDH-CES@N in ⅹ1.50k, and (f) LDH-CES@N in ⅹ4.00k.
(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of LDH-CES@N at different excitation wavelengths. Effects of incubation time (b), ionic strength (c), and pH (d) on fluorescent response.

(a) Fluorescent emission spectra of LDH-CES@N in the presence of OTC (0–60 μM). (b) Linear fitting curve of OTC concentration (1–60 μM) vs the intensity of variation values of the two emission peaks (F504+F616).

(a) The fluorescence intensity of LDH-CES@N upon the addition of TCs and various types of antibiotics. Inset: corresponding fluorescent photograph under 365 nm UV lamp light. (b) The fluorescence intensity of LDH-CES@N in the presence of interference. (c) Calculated molecular orbital energy levels of HOMO and LUMO for antibiotics.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of OTC and fluorescence emission spectra of LDH-C and LDH-CES@N. (b) Fluorescence lifetime of LDH-C and LDH-C-OTC. (c) The HOMO and LUMO energy levels of CIT and OTC. (d) Fluorescence lifetime of LDH-CES@N and LDH-CES@N-OTC.

(a) Schematic of the dark box device. (b) The fluorescence color under the UV lamp after adding different concentrations of OTC. (c) The linear relationship between R/B ratio and OTC concentrations. (d) Comparison of the OTC concentration detected by HPLC, fluorescence, and visual detection method.
Comparison of methods for the detection of TCs residues.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ZnS: Cu QDs: Copper-doped zinc sulfide quantum dots; FS@UIO66: Encapsulating fluorescent molecules in UIO66; UiO-66-NH2/Eu3+: Eu3+-functionalized regular octahedral UiO-66-NH2 material; Ag NCs: Silver nanoclusters; Eu-CDs: Ratio fluorescent probe based on citric acid and europium nitrate; Eu3+/NH2-MIL-53(Al): Eu3+ doped nanocomposites with NH2-MIL-53(Al) nanosheets

head.ce:title: Portable and real-time detection for tetracycline antibiotics using europium-doped LDH gel intercalated graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Protection, Development and Utilization of Medicinal Resources in Liupanshan Area, Ministry of Education, College of Pharmacy, Ningxia Medical University, Yinchuan 750004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Protection, Development and Utilization of Medicinal Resources in Liupanshan Area, Ministry of Education, College of Pharmacy, Ningxia Medical University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tetracyclines (TCs) residues pose a significant threat to the aquatic environment and human health, therefore this study aims to develop a simple, rapid, and sensitive TCs detection method. Herein, a dual-responsive gel probe (LDH-CES@N) was designed, consisting of the intercalation of graphene quantum dots into europium-doped layered double hydroxide (LDH). In the presence of TCs, the as-prepared probe exhibited dual emission fluorescence at 504 nm and 616 nm due to the synergistic effect of aggregation-induced emission and antenna effect. Meanwhile, the density functional theory was employed to validate the mechanism underlying TC-induced electron transfer from graphene quantum dots. The dual-signal response fluorescence probe has excellent detection ability of oxytetracycline, including a wide detection range (0–60 μM), low detection limit (0.145 μM), and rapid response time (120 s). Furthermore, combined with the smartphone, a portable and real-time detection platform was established for the visual detection of oxytetracycline in tap water and honey samples with desirable recovery rates (97.8 %-105.4 %). Therefore, this work provides a new strategy for fluorescence detection of trace pollutants, demonstrating considerable practical application potential.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracyclines (TCs) are the most widely veterinary drugs globally, employed in livestock production for treating infections and as feed additives to promote growth [1]. However, the overuse and misuse of TCs have resulted in significant residues in wastewater and animal-source foods [2–4]. Some reports have indicated that natural water bodies and edible honey worldwide are extensively and persistently exposed to environmental antibiotic residues, which pose serious risks, including toxicity, allergic reactions, bacterial resistance, and other adverse effects [5–9]. Therefore, the development of accurate rapid and convenient methods for detecting TCs residues is of great significance.

In recent years, fluorescence analysis has been widely utilized for TCs detection due to the simple operation, fast response, and low instrument cost [10,11]. Lanthanide metal ions are particularly valuable in fluorescence probe detection because of their high luminescence quantum yield, long fluorescence lifetime, and large Stokes shift [12–14]. Specifically, the Eu3 + coordination polymer has excellent fluorescence emission characteristics. Based on the β-diketone structure of TCs, using it as a sensitizer to coordinate lanthanide Eu3+ through the "antenna effect (AE)" can enhance the energy of Eu3+, and thus obtain the Eu-TCs complex with luminescent characteristics [15–18]. However, Eu3+ is coordinatively unsaturated, the vibration caused by the high-frequency -OH of coordinating water can quench the fluorescence. To reduce the effect of unsaturated coordination on fluorescence, citric acid (CIT) was selected as the ancillary ligand to eliminate the coordinated water molecules around Eu3+, resulting in a significant enhancement of fluorescence intensity [19–23].

Layered double hydroxide (LDH) is a class of inorganic two-dimensional clay materials composed of a positively charged layer and an interlayer equilibrium of anions and water molecules [24–28]. Due to its excellent anion exchange properties, CIT can easily intercalate the LDH layers to prepare graphene quantum dots (GQDs) through hydrothermal carbonization method [29,30]. Interestingly, Eu3+ can also replace the cations on the surface of LDH layers, enabling the construction of dual-emission fluorescent probes [31]. In this configuration, GQDs, and Eu3+, are firmly fixed on the LDH host, making them less likely to escape into a complex detection environment. This stabilization enhances fluorescence emission intensity, thereby improving the sensitivity, durability, and accuracy of TCs detection.

Unfortunately, LDH itself has the disadvantages of easy aggregation and poor water solubility, which hinders the actual fluorescence detection application greatly. Bonding LDH sheets to macromolecular polymers is considered a promising solution. As a polymer with the 3D network, hydrogel has been used to encapsulate various bio-molecules and nanoparticles for detecting various pollutants. The abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface of LDH provide the possibility for LDH silylation and subsequent preparation of hydrogel [32]. The hydrogel synthetic strategy improves dispersion and stability in water of LDH, offering a high specific surface area and then heightening the fluorescence effect.

Inspired by the above studies, this work aims to synthesize a dual-responsive fluorescence probe derived from Eu-doped LDH gel intercalated with GQDs (LDH-CES@N) for efficient detection of TCs from water and food samples. The "limited space" between the LDH layers constrained the movement of TCs, which can trigger aggregation-induced emission (AIE). The dual emission characteristics of LDH-CES@N are activated by TCs through AIE and AE at 504 nm and 616 nm, respectively, under single excitation at 360 nm. Silanized LDH can be used as crosslinking agents to in situ synthesize gel polymers ( Fig. 1). Taking oxytetracycline (OTC) as the analyte, the conditions for fluorescence detection of LDH-CES@N were investigated, and the mechanism of TCs-induced electron transfer was studied by density functional theory (DFT). Besides, to meet practical application needs, a portable and real-time detection platform was built by combining the smartphone with 96-well plate. Therefore, the established method provides an innovative approach for visual detection of TCs, demonstrating the potential for real-time detection of trace pollutants from complex matrices.

Sodium citrate, Al(NO3)3∙9H2O, NaOH, Eu(NO3)3∙6H2O, phosphoric acid (HPLC grade) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Mg(NO3)2∙6H2O, NH4OH, 3-methacryloxypropyltrimethoxysilane (MPTS), N-isopropyl acrylamide (N-iso), ammonium persulphate, tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), chlortetracycline (CTC) were purchased from Energy Chemical Co., Ltd. Acetonitrile (HPLC grade) were purchased from the Thermo Fisher Scientific (Shanghai, China) Instruments.

Absorption spectra were captured by UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence analysis was conducted using a FL-7100 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The Hitachi S-3400 N scanning electron microscope (SEM) and the Hitachi H-7650 transmission electron microscope (TEM) was used to characterize the surface structure and micro-morphology. The Bruker IFS-55 spectrophotometer was used to perform Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) analysis. The Ultima IV (Rigaku, Japan) powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD) was fitted with Cu Kα radiation from 5 to 85°. The ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fischer, USA) was used to record X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS). The conditions of HPLC-UV and LC-MS/MS analysis were described in the Supplementary materials part.

The scheme for the synthesis of LDH-CES@N is illustrated in Fig. 1. Initially, LDH was intercalated with CIT by co-precipitation method, followed by the in situ hydrothermal synthesis of LDH intercalated with GQDs based on CIT (LDH-C). Next, Eu3+ was incorporated into the LDH layers through cation exchange, forming LDH-CE. Subsequently, alkoxysilane from MPTS with -OH on the LDH surface, resulted in silanized LDH (LDH-CES). Finally, a gel polymer, LDH-CES@N, was prepared via polymerization using LDH-CES as the crosslinking agent and N-iso as the functional monomer.

Adjustments were made on the basis of previously reported studies [33]. A mixture of 0.9 M Mg(NO3)2∙6H2O and 0.3 M Al(NO3)3∙9H2O was slowly added to 0.32 M sodium citrate solution, and the pH was adjusted to about 10. Once the system stabilized, the reaction temperature was increased to 60 °C for 12 h. The white precipitates were collected by centrifugation and washed until neutral with distilled water. They were then dispersed in 50 mL distilled water and 5 mL NH4OH, followed by hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C for 8 h. After the reaction, the precipitates were washed to neutral and dried at 60 °C for 12 h.

Lanthanide cation was incorporated into LDH-C by cation replacement. Firstly, the LDH-C (0.5 g) was uniformly dispersed within 25 mL of methanol, followed by the addition of 0.2 mmol of Eu(NO₃)₃∙6H₂O. Then, the precipitates were collected and washed three times with ethanol and water. Finally, the products were dried at 60 °C for 12 h.

LDH-CE of 0.5 g was dispersed in a mixture of 20 mL ethanol and 10 mL MPTS and the solution was stirred under nitrogen protection for 2 h. The unreacted MPTS was washed with ethanol and the synthesized products were dried at 60 °C for 6 h.

LDH-CES (0.3 g) was dispersed in 6 mL water, and the same mass of N-iso was added. After thorough mixing, ammonium persulfate (catalyst) was added. Following 10 min of ultrasonic treatment, the products were polymerized at 60 °C for 12 h, resulting in milky gels. Then they were washed with distilled water, and dried by freeze-drying for 12 h.

The suspension was prepared by adding 10 mg LDH-CES@N into 10 mL water and ultrasonic for 15 min until fully dispersed. Various concentrations of OTC solution (400 μL) were added to 600 μL of the above suspension, and incubated at room temperature stable. The dual-emission spectra of fluorescence were measured at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of OTC in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the feasibility of this method, tap water and honey were selected as representative samples [21,34]. The physicochemical characteristics of these samples were listed in Table S1. The tap water sample was sourced from the laboratory's domestic supply. Impurities were eliminated through centrifugation, and the supernatant was subsequently filtered using a 0.45 μm membrane filter. This resulting filtrate was utilized for further experiments. The honey sample was purchased from the local markets. Honey samples (1 g) were diluted with 10 mL of PBS (pH 7.4) and then centrifuged to remove solid suspended matter. The supernatant was then filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane filter. Finally, three concentration levels of OTC (5, 10, and 20 μM) were added to the above-mentioned samples for fluorescence detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of OTC using a 96-well plate
ce:section.ce:para: The resultant LDH-CES@N was added to absolute ethanol and completely dispersed by sonication. A volume of 300 μL was transferred to each well slot of a 96-well plate and placed in an oven. Upon complete evaporation of ethanol, a probe film was formed in each well. Different concentrations of OTC standard solutions were added into the empty tank and then placed in the UV lamp light-dark box. The 96-well plates were further photographed and analyzed by a smartphone software. In this study, RGB values were selected for different concentrations of OTC to establish a linear relationship.

In this study, Gaussian 09 was used to perform structural optimization and frequency calculation within the B3LYP/6–31(g)d base set. After that, Multiwfn 3.8 (dev) (Last update: 2024-Nov-13) was used to calculate the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy, HOMO-LUMO gap, electrostatic potential (ESP) and investigate intermolecular weak interactions [35,36]. Finally, the above contents were graphically displayed through VMD 1.9.3, which was owned by the Theoretical and Computational Biophysics Group, NIH Center for Macromolecular Modeling and Bioinformatics, at the Beckman Institute, University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign [37].

Herein, the synthesis process was optimized by selecting the material concentration (3 mg mL−1) and a concentration of OTC (100 μM). The key factors influencing fluorescence intensity including the moles of sodium citrate, moles of Eu3+, volume ratio of ethanol to MPTS, and mass of N-iso were investigated. Initially, LDH intercalated with sodium citrate was obtained by anion exchange, and GQDs intercalation of LDH was obtained by hydrothermal reaction, while GQDs between layers are of great significance in improving the aggregation-induced luminescence of OTC. Therefore, the effect of 0–20 mmol of sodium citrate on fluorescence was studied. The fluorescence intensity at 504 nm was the highest when the moles of sodium citrate was 16 mmol ( Fig. 2a). Thus, the constructed LDH-C provides a limited and ordered micro-environment for the TCs, restricting the movement of TCs between the layers and enhancing the AIE response [33]. Then, considering the unique atomic-level distribution of metal cations in LDH, Eu3+ was exchanged into the layered plate of LDHs via cation replacement. As a result, the AE was produced at 616 nm, thereby constructing the second emitting signal of the dual-response probe. Since the incorporation of Eu3+ affects its chelation with OTC, the influence of Eu3+ within the range of 0.1–0.5 mmol was studied [31]. Given the synchronous response of the dual-signal, when the moles of the doped Eu3+ reach 0.2 mmol, the designed sensor system cannot only effectively transfer the energy of the OTC using AE to the Eu3+, but also show a stronger response at 504 nm (Fig. 2b). After that, the -OH groups on the LDH surface can react with the Si-O groups on the MPTS surface through a hydrolysis reaction to form the LDH-CES, which can be used as a crosslinking agent for gel synthesis [32]. In the 30 mL system, the volume ratio of ethanol to MPTS is 1:5, 1:2, 1:1, 2:1, and 5:1, respectively, which was used as the medium to functionalize LDH. When the volume ratio of ethanol to MPTS was 2:1, the silanization was relatively complete (Fig. 2c). Finally, a composite gel was synthesized using LDH-CES as a crosslinking agent and N-iso as a functional monomer. N-iso is the key factor that determines whether a gel forms, so the effect of N-iso mass from 0.2 to 0.5 g was studied to select a typical probe with good gel properties and optimal fluorescence response. As shown in Fig. 2d, when the mass is 0.3 g, the best state of gel synthesis is achieved. Based on the above, a gel probe was prepared in situ using functionalized LDH as a crosslinking agent, with 504 nm and 616 nm as dual responsive signals for sensitive detection of OTC.

The FT-IR, XRD, and XPS were employed to characterize the materials formed in each step. The FT-IR spectra demonstrate the characteristic functional groups during the synthesis ( Fig. 3a). In LDH-C, the peak at 1384 cm−1 is the infrared characteristic peak of C-O, 1616 cm−1 and 3491 cm−1 is attributed to the blending vibration, and stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups from the water molecules intercalated between LDH layers, respectively. And the characteristic peak near 611 cm−1 is due to the metal-oxygen stretching vibration [33]. The infrared spectrum did not change significantly after europium incorporation. After silanization, the peak near 1384 cm−1 becomes strong and sharp. In addition, the newly formed bands between 1551 cm−1 and 1651 cm−1 are observed in the IR spectrum of LDH-CES@N, which is attributed to the typical double peaks at the amide Ⅰ and amide II bands [32].

The crystal structure was confirmed and demonstrated by XRD (Fig. 3b). The obvious triple diffraction peaks of LDH-C, LDH-CE, and LDH-CES are observed at 11.58°, 23.18°, and 34.98°, which are attributed to the (003), (006) and (009) characteristic crystal planes of LDHs, respectively [24]. The results indicate that cation exchange and silanization occur only on the surface of the LDH layers, without affecting the overall crystal structure of LDH. Using LDH-CES as a crosslinking agent, LDH-CES@N obtained through free radical polymerization retains the characteristic peaks of LDH, suggesting that the gel retains the parent structure of LDH.

The elemental composition and distribution of functional groups were investigated by high-resolution XPS spectra. As illustrated in Fig. 3c, three primary elements (C, N, O) are identified in the LDH-CES@N from the comprehensive XPS spectrum. Fig. 3d-i presents the surface element valence to each step of material synthesis. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum obtained from the LDH-C layer reveals three peaks (Fig. 3d) at 284.8 eV for C-C, 286.0 eV for CO, and 288.6 eV for O-CO, which are attributed to the -COOH group of CIT intercalated within the LDH. The O 1 s spectra shown in Fig. 3e reveal two peaks at about 531.8 eV and 533.9 eV, corresponding to the CO and H2O, associated with CIT and water molecules between the LDHs. Additionally, the high-resolution N 1 s spectrum shows a single peak (Fig. 3f) at 406.7 eV, corresponding to NO3 - located between the LDH. After loading europium, in Eu 3d spectra decompose into four distinct peaks related to the Eu 3d5/2 and Eu 3d3/2 chemical states of Eu2+ (1124.8 eV and 1155.8 eV) and Eu3+ (1135.0 eV and 1164.6 eV), thus proving that Eu has been successfully incorporated into the LDH layers (Fig. 3g). The peak observed at 286.3 eV in the LDH-CES is ascribed to a C-O-C bond originating from MPTS (Fig. 3h). Furthermore, the high-resolution N 1 s spectrum from the LDH-CES@N layer contains two peaks (Fig. 3i), corresponding to C-N = C at 399.5 eV and -NH- at 400.7 eV, attributed to functional monomer N-iso groups. Collectively, these characterizations provide further confirmation of the successful synthesis of LDH-CES@N.

The morphology of the materials formed in each step obtained can be clearly observed through electron microscopy. In Fig.S1a, LDH has a lamellar structure and a smooth surface. When GQDs are intercalated, the TEM image of LDH-C is evident that the GQDs are intercalated and dispersed within the layered structure of the LDH (Fig.S1b). Fig. 4a depicts the image of LDH-C, which still retains the clear and uniform layered structure of the precursor LDH. As shown in Fig. 4b, LDH-CE exhibits a rough surface and obvious agglomeration but still retains the structure of LDH. In Fig.S2, the LDH-CE was mainly composed of C, O, Mg, Al, and Eu with a mass percentage of 21.83 %, 46.40 %, 16.53 %, 12.24 %, and 3.00 %, respectively. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 4c, the corresponding elemental mapping images show a homogeneous distribution, further confirming the successful doping of Eu. Fig. 4d presents the image of LDH-CES, which appears to have a smoother surface due to the modification by MPTS. Finally, the microscopic morphology of the LDH-CES@N composite material obtained through polymerization can be clearly seen in Fig. 4e-f. The freeze-dried gel material shows a loose and porous spongy surface. The rough surface provides more detection sites for the target analytes, while the porous structure facilitates the mass transfer channel, thereby making the detection more rapid and sensitive.

The emission and excitation properties of LDH-CES@N were explored and optimized in this work. As shown in Fig. 5a, when the excitation wavelength is increased from 330 nm to 390 nm, the intensity of emission peaks at LDH-CES@N with OTC at 504 nm and 616 nm cannot increase with the increase of the excitation wavelength but reaches its maximum value at 360 nm. The results illustrate that the emission peak is independent of the excitation wavelength, which may reduce the interference of self-fluorescence in the sensing process. Therefore, under 360 nm excitation, the emission spectrum of LDH-CES@N with OTC consists of two parts: a broad emission band at 504 nm induced by the AIE of OTC, and a narrow emission peak at 616 nm caused by the AE between OTC and Eu3+.

As shown in Fig. 5b, after the reaction of LDH-CES@N with OTC, the intensity of two emission peaks (F504+F616) rose rapidly and leveled off after 120 s, after which the intensity cannot change significantly with increasing incubation time. The results indicate that the reaction can reach equilibrium within 120 s. In addition, inorganic salts are important components that are often present in water. The NaCl solutions (0–0.5 M) were investigated to study the influence of ionic strength on the fluorescence response. As shown in Fig. 5c, changes in NaCl concentration did not significantly alter the fluorescence intensity at 504 nm and 616 nm. The pH value is a critical factor influencing the fluorescence response. Given the inherent properties of LDH, it exhibits instability in strongly acidic conditions, leading to dissolution that compromises its crystal and interlayer structures [38]. This disruption adversely affects the stability of the intercalated GQDs and consequently impacts the AIE effect. Conversely, LDHs are recognized as alkaline carriers. Thus, elevated pH levels are advantageous for maintaining structural integrity. However, excessive alkalinity may result in the leaching of Eu3+, which subsequently influences the AIE effect. As illustrated in Fig. 5d, LDH-CES@N demonstrates enhanced stability at a pH of 7. Considering OTC heightened sensitivity to alkaline solution, subsequent experiments were conducted under neutral pH conditions.

To explore the potential of LDH-CES@N in OTC detection, fluorescence experiments with different concentrations of OTC were performed. As shown in Fig. 6a, the fluorescence intensity of LDH-CES@N is weak in the absence of OTC. However, the fluorescence signals at 504 nm and 616 nm display an obvious enhancement with the increased concentration of OTC. In Fig. 6b, a good linear relationship between fluorescence intensity (F504+F616) and OTC concentration (0–60 μM) is obtained. The linear equation is y = 4.498x+ 47.763 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9935. Furthermore, the detection limit (LOD) is obtained at 0.145 μM, which is lower than the maximum residue limit set by the European Union (217 nM) [39]. The LOD was obtained based on Eq. (1):
(1)
LOD =
3 σ
s
Where σ represents the standard deviation of LDH-CES@N blank signal and s is the slope of the standard curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference performance for TCs detection
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the specific selectivity of the dual-signal probe toward TCs, the fluorescence responses to various antibiotics, including sulfadiazine (SUL), sulfathiazole (SUTH), chloramphenicol (CAP), florfenicol (FF), penicillin (PEN), metronidazole (MET), isoniazid (ISO) were investigated in this study. As shown in Fig. 7a, other types of antibiotics do not trigger the fluorescence response of LDH-CES@N, except for the TCs. It can be explained that the dual-responsive fluorescence signal is based on the AIE luminescence of TCs and the AE between TCs and Eu3+. Moreover, under a 365 nm UV lamp, TCs can be easily distinguished from other antibiotics by visually distinct colors. Various common interfering substances including glucose, lysine (Lys), tyrosine (Tyr), proline (Pro), isoleucine (Iso), glutamate (Glu), Mg2+, Al3+, Na+, K+, SO4 2-, CO3 2-, PO4 3-, Cl-, Br-, NO3 -, S2O8 2-, CH3COO-, B(OH)4 -, CAP, SUTH, PEN, MET at a concentration of 0.1 M were selected to investigate the influence on the fluorescence response of TCs. As illustrated in Fig. 7b, the presence of interfering substances does not affect the fluorescence response signals of LDH-CES@N. Meanwhile, by adding the OTC in the interferents, the fluorescence responses of these interfering substances cannot undergo significant changes when compared with that of TCs with the same concentration. This reveals the potential of LDH-CES@N in the practical detection of complex sample matrices.

Using frontier molecular orbital theory can provide an initial exploration of the reason for high selectivity [40]. The LUMO and HOMO indicate the ability to accept and donate electrons, respectively. For the same base set, the energy difference between LUMO and HOMO, known as the band gap, indicates how easily the molecule can be excited. The smaller the band gap, the easier it will be to excite. In this study, since GQDs were prepared by hydrothermal carbonization of CIT intercalated between LDH layers, CIT was chosen to verify the mechanism. As shown in Fig. 7c, the CIT exhibits a higher LUMO level than that of TCs. Meanwhile, compared with other antibiotics, the smaller band gap of TCs makes it easier to excite, thus revealing outstanding selectivity for TCs.

In order to further analyze the detection mechanism between LDH-CES@N and TCs, a series of characteristic analyses and theoretical calculations were carried out. Since the UV–visible spectrum of OTC does not overlap with the fluorescence emission spectrum of the LDH-C and LDH-CES@N ( Fig. 8a), the interaction mechanism can exclude the internal filtering effect and fluorescence resonance energy transfer [41]. Due to the fact that OTC itself is considered an AIE molecule [42], the ordered and limited layered structure of LDH restricts its movement, thereby enhancing aggregation-induced. As shown in Fig. 8b, the average fluorescence lifetime of LDH-C at 504 nm increased from 0.61 ns to 2.51 ns after the addition of OTC, which is an unignorable dynamic change process. Then, the relative energies of the LUMO and HOMO of CIT and OTC were calculated as well. As shown in Fig. 8c, the LUMO and HOMO of CIT are −0.40 eV and −7.53 eV, and those of OTC are −2.39 eV and −6.28 eV, respectively. Since the LUMO of CIT is higher than that of OTC, the electron transfer from the LUMO of CIT to the LUMO of OTC confirms the possibility of the electron transformation (ET) process between CIT and OTC [43]. Finally, the fluorescence lifetime change of LDH-CES@N at 616 nm was explored. As shown in Fig. 8d, the average fluorescence lifetime of LDH-CES@N increases from 1.50 ns to 2.56 ns upon the addition of OTC. This dynamic process is due to the fact that the β-diketone structure of OTC could coordinate with Eu3+, and the energy is effectively transferred to Eu3+, which is the aforementioned AE.

The ESP of the molecular surface can predict the binding site of the intermolecular interaction, and its maximum and minimum values correspond to the yellow and green spheres, respectively [44–47]. The -OH group in the OTC structure has a maximum electrostatic potential (61.62 kcal mol−1) observed around the hydrogen atom ( Fig. 9a), and the -COOH structure in the CIT structure has a minimum electrostatic potential (-32.34 kcal mol−1) observed around the oxygen atom (Fig. 9b). It can be inferred that the bond between OTC and CIT may be through electrostatic attraction interaction.

The independent gradient model in DFT is used to explain the weak interaction between molecules [48,49]. In the isosurface between molecules, blue, green, and red regions represent the hydrogen bond interaction, van der Waals force, and steric hindrance, respectively. A distinct blue-green isosurface was observed between CIT and OTC (Fig. 9c). Meanwhile, there is an obvious blue peak at −0.03 in the scatter plot (Fig. 9d), indicating that strong hydrogen bond in the system. Thus, the most plausible mechanism for the ET process between CIT and OTC is due to the hydrogen bond [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted visual detection of OTC in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: Since fluorescence detection instruments are expensive and bulky, achieving portable and rapid detection of TCs remains challenging. In this study, a portable, cost-effective, and easily accessible dark box device for instant visual detection of TCs was developed. This device consists of a cardboard box serving as the dark box, a UV lamp (365 nm) as the excitation light source, a smartphone as the detection sensor, and a 96-well plate as a probe carrier ( Fig. 10a and Fig.S3). Under different concentrations of OTC, the 96-well plate showed a noticeable color shift (from blue to red), which can be easily distinguished by the naked eye (Fig. 10b). Furthermore, these images were transformed into data by using a smartphone, which the OTC level was quantified through converting the light signal into RGB values. Furthermore, these images were transformed into data by using the color recognition app on the smartphone. Thus, a visual detection method based on smartphones was established, which is convenient to use and less susceptible to environmental and other factors. When color information is extracted from images using the smartphone, selecting the central area can improve the accuracy of image analysis. In order to study the effect of smartphone camera quality on visual detection, three different smartphone image processing applications were compared. As shown in Table S2, the color information extracted by various image processing programs is consistent, indicating that different image processing applications have no impact on RGB extraction. By selecting the red (R) and blue (B) channels, the conversion value was calculated by ratio. As shown in Fig. 10c, R/B is linearly related to OTC concentration in the range of 0–40 μM (R2=0.9857), with the linear regression equation y = 0.0382x+ 0.1964.

To validate the reliability and usefulness of LDH-CES@N, the visual detection method was employed to analyze the TCs residual in actual tap and honey samples. Combined with LC-MS/MS identification results, it was found that there was no OTC was detected in selected test samples (Fig.S4-S5). Thus, the accuracy of the detection method was conducted by spiked recovery experiments as shown in Table S3. The spiked recovery values range from 97.8 % to 105.4 % with RSDs < 4.6 %. To further verify the reliability of the method, the HPLC-UV was used to measure the concentration of OTC in actual samples. Compared with both HPLC-UV and fluorescence methods, visual detection reveals considerable analytical effectiveness (Fig. 10d). The results confirmed that this study has proposed a portable and instant OTC-sensitive detection platform, providing technical support for the on-site qualitative identification and semi-quantitative detection of TCs.

A comparative study with previously reported fluorescence detection methods was also conducted in this work. As shown in Table 1, notable advantages are exhibited in terms of LOD and linear range, while having a reasonable response time. In addition, a portable and straightforward visualization sensing device established in this study can provide a fast and sensitive analytical strategy without being limited by experimental instruments and detection environment. Therefore, the established method shows an innovative approach for visual detection TCs, indicating a promising potential trace pollutant analysis from complex matrices.

In this work, a novel fluorescent probe (LDH-CES@N) was successfully developed for the synchronous fluorescence detection of TCs with dual emission capabilities. The dual emission characteristics of LDH-CES@N were activated at 504 nm and 616 nm in the presence of TCs due to AIE and AE effects, enhancing selectivity and eliminating background interference greatly. This approach offers a wider detection range (0–60 μM), lower LOD (0.145 μM), and rapid response time (120 s). Besides, the DFT was used to investigate the fluorescence mechanism underlying TC-induced electron transfer. Combined with the smartphone and 96-well plate, a portable and real-time detection platform was developed for achieving sensitive detection of OTC in tap water and honey samples equipped with satisfactory recovery rates. Therefore, this method showcased excellent feasibility and applicability, highlighting the substantial potential for detecting trace analytes in complex samples.

In this study, a novel dual-signal fluorescent probe LDH-CES@N was synthesized, and a simple fluorescence sensing platform was established based on it. Due to the dual emission ability of LDH-CES@N, the dual signal response showed significant emission at 504 nm and 616 nm, effectively eliminating background interference and improving the selectivity for TCs. The method demonstrated good feasibility and applicability for detecting antibiotic residues in complex samples, providing a simple, fast, and real-time visualized application prospect for food safety and environmental monitoring.

Xiaoqin Hai: Investigation. Hongbo Li: Resources, Methodology, Investigation. Yongpeng Ma: Investigation. Jiawei Liu: Validation, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation. Ruiyu Fu: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation. Xin Di: Supervision, Resources, Project administration. Xiaoli Guo: Supervision, Project administration. Xiaojia Niu: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (82404319), Natural Science Foundation of Ningxia Province (2023AAC02040 and 2023AAC02034) and the Key Research and Development Program of Ningxia Province, China (2023BSB03045) and Major Science and Technology Project of Ningxia Medical University (Unveiling and Leading Project), China (XJKF240320).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2025.137143.
